notes: The compilation is impressively well-suited to a sparse rule ("Be Civil") that required the compiler to lean on the moderator's fuller description. The use of a single subjective item with a well-calibrated threshold, detailed rubric, and atmosphere note demonstrates good judgment. The main structural observation is that the anchor cannot be verified against the compiler-visible rule text ("Be Civil"), but it perfectly matches the moderator's ground truth description. The examples_to_include field is set to 5 but only 3 examples were generated — a minor implementation gap. Overall, this is a high-quality compilation for a deliberately vague rule.

The fundamental problem with this compilation is that it only addresses the scoping condition (Official Discussion threads) and entirely omits the core enforcement condition (140-character minimum). The compiled tree, as-is, can only determine whether a post is in scope — it cannot flag any violations. A complete checklist would need at minimum: (1) Is this a top-level comment? (2) Is it in an Official Discussion thread? (3) Is the comment at least 140 characters long? The anchor is drawn from the moderator description rather than the rule text shown to the compiler (which is just a title). The borderline example misapplies a quality judgment where a purely mechanical length rule applies.

The compilation appears to have been severely truncated or suffered a generation error. The rule text provided to the compiler was just "Posting guidelines" (2 words) rather than the full rule text, which likely caused the compiler to produce incomplete output. All three checklist items are exact duplicates of each other. Meanwhile, the generated examples correctly reflect the full rule intent (covering blog spam, illegal downloads, and vote manipulation), suggesting the example generator had access to more context than the checklist compiler. The overall tree is not production-ready: it covers only 1 of 4 substantive requirements, and even that is duplicated 3 times.

The most significant issue is the logical ambiguity between the item description ("lack both") and the rubric (OR logic). The rule clearly requires BOTH to be present, so removing a post that has an artist name but no song title should be a violation — the rubric handles this correctly, but the description does not. This discrepancy could cause inconsistent application. Additionally, the rule_text_anchor cites a phrase not present in the compiler-visible rule text ("Song Posting Requirements"), though it accurately reflects the ground truth moderator description. The atmosphere_influenced flag and atmosphere_note are interesting additions but not part of the standard checklist schema — they may cause parsing issues in production.

The main issue is the anchor, which references the moderator's ground-truth description ("Don't inject opinion or hyperbole into link titles") rather than any phrase actually present in the compiler-visible rule text ("No Opinion in Link Titles"). Since the compiler only saw the short title, they appear to have used the extended description as the anchor source — which is technically incorrect per anchor accuracy criteria, even though the intent is sound. Everything else about this compilation is high quality: the rubric is detailed and practical, the examples are well-chosen, and the single-item structure is appropriately minimal for a simple rule.

The most critical issue is that the compiler produced three completely identical checklist items. This appears to be a generation bug/error rather than intentional design. The result is that: (a) two of the three requirements (unapproved tag usage, missing tag when required) are not covered by any distinct checklist item; (b) the same check would be applied three times redundantly. The rule text as shown to the compiler was just "Post Tag Usage" — a very thin title — but the moderator's full description is rich. The compiler apparently had access to the full description (given the detailed rubric in the prompt_template), yet still generated triplicates. The examples are better-crafted than the checklist itself, referencing items that should exist but don't.

The most significant issue is the regex logic: `(?:https?://)?(?:www\.)?reddit\.com/r/[A-Za-z0-9_]+` would match `np.reddit.com/r/...` because it only anchors on `reddit.com`, not the subdomain. A corrected pattern should use a negative lookahead or explicitly anchor on non-np subdomains, e.g., `(?:https?://)?(?:www\.)?(?!np\.)reddit\.com/r/[A-Za-z0-9_]+`. The anchor accuracy issue is also notable: the anchor quotes the moderator description rather than the compiler-visible rule text ("NP Links Only").

The core problem with this compiled checklist is that the rule text provided to the compiler was extremely sparse ("Fresh Fridays Rule" — just a title), forcing the compiler to rely entirely on the moderator's description. The anchor correctly quotes the description but cannot be a verbatim substring of the provided rule text. More critically, the structural logic is incomplete (missing the Friday day-of-week check) and logically inverted (checks for presence of valid flair rather than absence, while the description says "lacks"). The tree in its current form would not function correctly as an automated moderation rule.

The main concern is the anchor accuracy issue: the rule text field contains only "Interviews Title Guidelines" (a heading), while the anchor quotes the full rule description that only appears in the moderator's ground truth. The compiler clearly had access to more information than the "rule text" field shows, which creates a mismatch in anchor evaluation. The compiled checklist itself is high quality — the rubric is thorough and well-aligned with the rule's intent. The atmosphere_note referencing a sibling "No Opinion in Link Titles" rule is a thoughtful addition. The single-item flat structure is appropriate for this simple rule.

rule_text_short: Do Not Post Featured Thread Content
notes: The compiler did an excellent job consolidating a multi-part rule into a single, well-specified subjective item. The prompt_template effectively encodes all seven thread types with scheduling details. The main weakness is in example quantity and diversity — only 3 examples were generated despite requesting 5, and they only illustrate 3 of the 7 thread types. More examples testing Moronic Monday (basic questions), RIYL (recommendation requests), Daily Discussion (hypotheticals), and Monthly Feedback Thread would strengthen the example set. The atmosphere note is a nice addition providing useful context about the moderation philosophy.

The fundamental issue with this compiled rule is that "No Songs from Favorites List" likely refers to a specific, enumerated list (perhaps pinned in the subreddit or associated with hiphop101) rather than a general judgment about whether a song is "canonical" or "classic." The compiler has transformed what could be a deterministic check (is this song on List X?) into a subjective judgment (is this song a widely-recognized classic?). This is a major logical/interpretive flaw. The moderator description ("Check out hiphop101 if you're into this stuff") suggests there's a specific resource being referenced. The checklist would be more accurate if it directed moderators to check against a specific list rather than making a general quality judgment about song popularity/canonicity.

The most critical issue is the exact duplication of the checklist item — both entries are character-for-character identical. This appears to be a compilation error. Additionally, the anchors reference text from the moderator's description rather than the actual rule text visible to the compiler ("No Frequent/Reposted Content"), which is very short and doesn't contain the quoted anchor text. The checklist also fails to create a dedicated item for the "Done to Death" FAQ requirement, folding it into the song-repost rubric instead. The overall approach of using a single subjective item to handle the entire rule is understandable given the vague rule text, but the implementation has significant flaws.

rule_text_short: No Images and Vines Without Exceptions
notes: The most significant issue is the anchor accuracy: the compiler was given "No Images and Vines Without Exceptions" as the rule text, but the anchor quotes the moderator's full description. This suggests the compiler had access to the full description when compiling but used the wrong text as the anchor source. Functionally, the checklist is excellent — the single item correctly and clearly implements the rule with all exceptions handled. The five-example promise in the template was only partially fulfilled (3 of 5 delivered).

The compiled checklist is otherwise excellent — the single-item design is appropriate given the rule's simplicity, the rubric is detailed and actionable, and the examples are well-chosen. The main deficiency is the anchor inaccuracy: the compiler appears to have sourced the anchor from outside the rule text they were given (likely from the moderator description), resulting in a verbatim mismatch with the actual rule text "No Bashing Threads." If the moderator description were made available to the compiler as ground truth, this would be acceptable, but as presented it is an inaccuracy. Additionally, the rule text itself ("No Bashing Threads") is extremely sparse, which means the compiler had to exercise significant interpretive judgment — and did so sensibly by referencing the fuller moderator description implicitly.

rule_text_short: No Social Media Requests for Upvotes
notes: Overall, this is a well-compiled checklist for a simple, single-condition rule. The main areas for improvement are: (1) generating the full 5 examples as specified in the rubric configuration, and (2) ensuring at least one borderline example that hinges on the ambiguity of the external social media post's language rather than the Reddit post body itself. The potential ban consequence is appropriately noted in atmosphere metadata rather than as a separate checklist condition, which is a reasonable design choice.

The most critical issues are: (1) The checklist tree contains a complete duplicate of its only item — both nodes are identical. This appears to be a compiler/generation bug. (2) The rule's requirement that ALL tweets must be self posts (link posts to tweets are not allowed) is not covered by any checklist item. (3) The anchor references the moderator's description rather than the rule text as seen by the compiler. The compiled checklist would need significant revision before being production-ready.

The main issue is the anchor accuracy: "Playlists are not allowed." does not appear verbatim in the compiler-visible rule text ("No Playlists"). The anchor appears to be sourced from the moderator's ground-truth description rather than the rule text itself. This could cause traceability issues in production. Everything else is well-constructed for such a simple rule.

The main issue is the anchor text mismatch: the compiler used "Birthday Threads are not allowed." as the anchor, which matches the moderator's description but not the literal rule text ("No Birthday Threads"). This is a minor but real discrepancy in anchor accuracy. Overall, the compiled checklist is well-constructed for a simple, single-requirement rule — the rubric is particularly thoughtful in distinguishing pure birthday threads from substantive posts that mention birthdays.

The rule text provided to the compiler ("Concert Review Standards") is extremely minimal — essentially just a title. The compiler appears to have had access to the moderator's full description (the ground truth) to construct the checklist, which is why the anchor matches the ground truth but not the rule text field. This is a metadata/input structuring issue rather than a compilation flaw. Overall, the compiled checklist is well-constructed given the vague source material, with an appropriate subjective item type, clear rubric, and good examples.

This is a well-compiled checklist for a very simple, single-clause rule. The atmosphere_note correctly captures the subreddit culture and the redirect destinations mentioned in the moderator's description. The compiled output is lean, accurate, and actionable. The only marginal improvement would be a second borderline example or a slightly more obviously compliant example (e.g., one that involves meeting a rapper but has substantial music discussion). Overall, this is a high-quality compilation.

The major issue with this compilation is the exact duplication of the single checklist item. The item itself is high quality — well-reasoned, clear, and correctly implements the rule — but having it appear twice is a significant error that would cause double-processing and confusion. The anchor accuracy issue (paraphrased rather than verbatim) is also notable given the rule text is only three words ("Official Streams Only"), making it trivial to quote correctly. Despite these structural problems, the substantive content of the checklist is excellent.

1. The anchor quotes from the moderator description rather than the compiler-visible rule text ("No Timed Posts"), resulting in a score of 1 for anchor accuracy — this is a fundamental issue with the compilation process.
2. The inclusion of upvote-milestone patterns (regex for "reaches/hits [number]") goes beyond the rule's stated intent, which is specifically about time-based countdown posts.
3. The borderline example, while creative, is likely actually violating under a straightforward reading of the rule, as the rule doesn't carve out exceptions based on legitimate intent.
4. The related_checklist_item_description fields in examples reference an item description ("Does the timed post pattern represent a genuine timed-post gimmick rather than a coincidental phrase?") that doesn't exist in the compiled tree, suggesting the examples were generated with a different or extended tree in mind.

The rule as given to the compiler ("No Snippets") is very brief, but the moderator's description is much richer. The compiler did a reasonable job inferring the full intent. The key gap is the explicit exception allowing snippet discussion within DD or broader-scope threads — this is mentioned in the rubric but should ideally be a formal checklist condition (or at minimum more prominently structured) to prevent false removals of legitimate discussion posts. The atmosphere_note is a nice touch acknowledging the community-specific context of audio quality and fakes.

This is a well-compiled checklist for a short, simple rule. The single-item design is appropriate given the rule's simplicity. The compiler has done an excellent job expanding the sparse rule text ("No Political Endorsements") into a nuanced, operable rubric that faithfully reflects the moderator's ground truth (interviews fine, bare endorsements not). The atmosphere note adds useful transparency about the threshold choice. The null related_checklist_item_description on the compliant example is a minor omission but does not affect evaluation quality.

The core issue with this compilation is the mismatch between the very brief rule text ("No Vague Announcements") and the moderator's specific ground truth intent ("A picture of 2 rappers together is not an announcement of an upcoming collaboration"). The compiler appears to have accessed or inferred the ground truth description and used it as the anchor, but the compiled checklist generalizes too broadly from a narrow, specific rule. The anchor accuracy failure (quoting ground truth instead of rule text) suggests the compiler mixed up the two sources. The checklist would benefit from being scoped more tightly to the specific scenario described in the ground truth, rather than generalizing to all forms of vague announcements.

The most critical issue with this compiled checklist is the exact duplication of the only checklist item — the same node appears twice at the root level. This would cause double-evaluation in any automated system and is a fundamental structural defect. Additionally, the anchor text is sourced from the moderator description rather than the compiler-visible rule text ("No Illegal Downloads"), making anchor accuracy score a 1. The rule itself is simple (one condition) and could be well-served by a single subjective item, but the duplication undermines the entire tree's usability.

The most significant issue is the anchor accuracy problem: the rule text visible to the compiler is simply "No Petitions," but the anchor quotes the moderator description text instead. The regex pattern including bare `petition\b` is also problematic for precision, as it would match many non-violating posts that merely discuss or mention petitions. The checklist would benefit from a second item or a note distinguishing between linking to/actively posting a petition versus incidentally mentioning one in discussion. The borderline example in the generated examples actually highlights this gap well, suggesting the compiler was aware of the ambiguity but did not resolve it in the checklist logic.

The anchor accuracy issue is the main concern: the rule_text_anchor quotes the moderator's full description ("Please do not post requests to buy or sell tickets.") rather than the actual rule text presented to the compiler ("No Ticket Sales"). The regex pattern is comprehensive but does not cover "trading," "swapping," or "exchanging" tickets, which could be minor gaps in deterministic coverage.

The main issue is the anchor accuracy: the compiler used the moderator's full description as the anchor source rather than the actual rule text ("No Ranking Questions"). The compiled checklist itself is well-constructed — the subjective scoring rubric is particularly thoughtful, with clear guidance on borderline cases. The atmosphere note about hiphopheads' strict enforcement culture is a useful contextual addition. The compilation appears to have gone severely wrong. The rule text "Civility" is a one-word stub, which may have caused the compiler to struggle. The result is: (1) three identical items instead of one, (2) only the personal insults requirement addressed despite the moderator's description containing four additional distinct requirements (racism, sexism, bigotry, Holocaust denialism), and (3) the anchors reference text not present in the compiler's visible rule text. The examples, paradoxically, are well-constructed and reference the missing requirements — suggesting the example generator had access to the full rule description but the checklist compiler did not (or failed to use it). This compilation is not production-ready and needs a complete rebuild with the full moderator description as input.

The rule text given to the compiler was simply "Scope" (a heading), while the actual rule content is in the moderator's description. The atmosphere note and threshold reasoning are thoughtful additions that improve operability. The single-item flat tree is a reasonable design choice for a scope gate rule.

The primary weakness is the anchor accuracy issue: the rule text visible to the compiler is only "No current events," yet the anchor quotes text from the moderator's full description. This is a systematic issue, though in practice the underlying logic and coverage are sound. The compiled checklist is otherwise very well constructed for a single-condition rule — the subjective scoring rubric, threshold justification, and borderline example handling are all commendable. The example set would benefit from a clearer "compliant" borderline (e.g., an event from exactly 20 years ago) rather than the medieval example, which is too far from the boundary to test it.

The compilation appears incomplete or erroneous in multiple ways: (1) The checklist is literally just one item duplicated, covering only one of several rule requirements. (2) The generated examples reference checklist item descriptions that don't exist in the actual checklist (e.g., the title vagueness check), suggesting the compiler may have intended more items but failed to include them. This tree would be non-functional as a production moderation tool — it would only flag off-topic posts and miss all title formatting violations.

The compiler did an impressive job fleshing out a very sparse rule text ("Homework") into a detailed, well-reasoned subjective checklist item with a thoughtful rubric. The main technical issue is the anchor accuracy — the quoted anchor text comes from the moderator's description rather than the compiler-visible rule text. The overall quality of the compiled tree is high given the minimal input. The single-item tree is appropriate for this rule, which is fundamentally about a single nuanced judgment.

The most critical issue is the exact duplication of the single checklist item. This appears to be a compilation bug where the same item was emitted twice. Additionally, the compiled checklist misses an explicit check for whether the NSFW tag is present — this is a key part of the rule (posts with NSFW *topics* are allowed if properly tagged, but should be deleted if the title itself is NSFW). A structural item checking `post.flair == "NSFW"` combined with the subjective title check would better implement the rule's two-part logic.

The most critical issue is the exact duplication of the two checklist items — this appears to be a compiler bug where the same item was generated twice. In a production environment, this would result in posts being evaluated twice with identical criteria, wasting resources and potentially double-counting violations. 

Additionally, the rule has a clear deterministic component (explicit keyword presence) that is missing as a dedicated deterministic item. A better structure would be: (1) a deterministic item checking for "most", "least", "best", "worst" in the question text, and (2) a subjective item for the "can be reworded" catch-all. This would provide faster, cheaper enforcement for the most obvious cases and reserve the subjective LLM evaluation for edge cases.

The most critical issue is the exact duplication of the single checklist item. The tree should be fixed by: (1) removing the duplicate, and (2) adding a deterministic item for the explicit trigger phrases named in the rule ("In your area of expertise", "examples of [X] throughout history", "What are some facts about [X]"). The example for es-1 references a checklist item for trigger phrases ("Does the post contain trigger phrases explicitly flagged by the rule?") that does not exist in the compiled tree — this inconsistency suggests the compiler intended to include such an item but it was omitted or lost. The rule's redirect suggestion (Friday-Free-for-All, Tuesday Trivia) is informational and does not require a checklist item.

The most critical issue is that the compiled checklist contains two completely identical items — this appears to be a compilation error (perhaps a copy-paste or generation bug). This renders the tree both redundant and incomplete, since "soapboxing" (lectures, theories, wall-of-text posts) is never distinguished from "loaded questions." The moderator description also includes requirements about comment quality (sourced, on-topic, not politically/religiously motivated) that are entirely absent, though the compiler only saw the short rule title "No 'Soapboxing' or Loaded Questions," which somewhat mitigates this gap. The rubric quality within the single effective item is actually quite good — the problem is structural duplication and insufficient coverage of the soapboxing dimension.

The compiled checklist is well-suited to this relatively simple, single-condition rule. The use of a detailed rubric within a single subjective item is an appropriate design choice for a rule where the boundary between factual and counterfactual questions can be subtle. The atmosphere_note correctly captures the r/HistoryWhatIf redirect recommendation even though it's not a separate checklist item. Overall, this is a high-quality compilation with only very minor gaps.

The compiled checklist is derived from a very minimal rule text ("Privacy") but clearly draws on the moderator's full description. The anchor accuracy issue stems from this disconnect — the anchor references text from the moderator description, not the one-word rule text. The tree is functionally reasonable but incomplete: it misses the military records/identification referral requirement and the moderator-discretion clause. The single-node structure with action "continue" and no children is also architecturally incomplete — it should presumably lead to a removal action rather than continuing to non-existent children. The example linked to the violating case references a "related_checklist_item_description" that does not match any actual checklist item's description, suggesting the example was written with a more comprehensive checklist in mind.

rule_text_short: Limitations on Questions Concerning Historical Artifacts and Items
notes: The compiled checklist is fundamentally broken: it contains only one unique check (duplicated twice) covering the narrowest sub-clause of the rule (appraisal for sale), while the primary substance of the rule — prohibiting questions about artifacts with potentially illegal/unethical/archaeologically unsound acquisition — is entirely absent. The checklist also lacks any exemption logic for family heirlooms or common collectibles. The "action: continue" with no parent node or final verdict means the tree would never produce a removal decision. This needs to be rebuilt almost from scratch. The examples, ironically, are quite good and demonstrate understanding of the rule's full intent — they reference checklist items that should exist but don't.

The root problem is that the rule text provided to the compiler was just the single word "Answers" — an extremely sparse prompt. The compiler apparently had to infer the full rule requirements. However, given the moderator's full description was available as ground truth, the compiled checklist fails badly on coverage: it addresses only the citation requirement while entirely omitting the core requirements of depth, explanatory quality, and historiographical standards. The triplication of the single item is a serious defect. The examples, interestingly, reference checklist items that don't appear in the compiled tree (e.g., depth/superficiality checks), suggesting the example generator had access to a richer conception of the rule than the checklist captures.

The compiled checklist appears to have been generated with a serious error: it contains only one unique item (referral link check) replicated three times, and fails to address the vast majority of the rule's requirements (Wikipedia-as-sole-source prohibition, sourcing encouragement, failure to substantiate when asked, bookseller link guidance, "you are not a source" principle, etc.). This tree would be nearly useless in practice for moderating r/AskHistorians sourcing standards. The examples, ironically, demonstrate a better understanding of the full rule than the compiled checklist does, since they test Wikipedia-only sourcing (a major requirement) even though no checklist item covers it.

The most fundamental problem with this compilation is that the rule — "Write an in-depth answer" — is primarily about answer quality, expertise, depth, and contextual explanation, which requires subjective checklist items. The compiler produced only a single deterministic bare-link check, triplicated identically. This makes the checklist almost entirely useless for enforcing the rule as written. A proper compilation would include subjective items such as: (1) Is the answer more than one or two sentences? (2) Does it provide context for unfamiliar readers? (3) Does it demonstrate expertise beyond simple web searching? (4) Does it cite sources or evidence? The triplication of the same item also suggests a possible generation/compilation bug.

The anchor accuracy issue is the main weakness: the rule_text_anchor quotes from the moderator's full description rather than the short rule text "No personal anecdotes" that the compiler was shown. In a real deployment where the rule text is the short version, the anchor would be inaccurate. However, the anchor content is substantively valuable — it quotes the full rationale which provides helpful context. If the compiler had access to the moderator description (ground truth) as part of the compilation process, this might be acceptable; but as evaluated strictly against the rule text shown to the compiler, it's a mismatch. Everything else about this compiled checklist is excellent: single well-crafted item, clear rubric, appropriate threshold, good atmosphere note, and high-quality diverse examples.

1. The compiled checklist is minimal (single node) and correctly implements the phrase-detection aspect of the rule, but misses the broader principle that answers based on personal opinion or supposition are disallowed even without explicit trigger phrases. The moderator description makes clear this is the underlying principle, with the phrases serving as warning indicators. A more complete checklist might add a subjective item for "does the answer present personal opinion or supposition without historical evidence, even absent explicit trigger phrases?" 2. The `related_checklist_item_description` in the violating and borderline examples references a checklist item ("Does the answer rely primarily on speculation or personal opinion rather than historical evidence or sourced knowledge?") that doesn't exist in the compiled tree — suggesting the example generator anticipated a richer checklist than was actually compiled. 3. The regex for "to my understanding" uses `\bto my understanding\b` — the word boundary after "understanding" may not fire if followed by a period, but most regex engines treat `\b` as a boundary before punctuation, so this should work correctly in practice.

The compiled checklist is lean and well-constructed for a rule that is fundamentally about authorial intent (partial vs. complete answers). The main weakness is the anchor accuracy issue — the anchor text was drawn from the moderator's full description rather than the compiler-visible rule text ("No partial answers or 'placeholders'"). The moderator description is richer and more useful as a source, but the anchor field is supposed to cite the rule text as seen by the compiler. Operationally, the checklist would function well in a moderation context; the rubric captures the spirit of all sub-clauses even if not every one is explicitly itemized.

The compiled checklist appears to be substantially incomplete — perhaps the compiler only submitted one of several intended items, and then that single item was accidentally duplicated in the output. The generated examples are actually more informative than the checklist itself, referencing a "political agenda" item that was supposed to be (but is not) in the tree. The rule's short title "No political agendas or moralising" and the full description both place heavy emphasis on agenda-driven writing and polemical tone, neither of which is captured in the existing checklist item. The checklist needs at minimum: (1) removal of the duplicate; (2) addition of an item about political agenda / personal advocacy; (3) addition of an item about moralising / polemical tone; (4) possibly an item about good-faith/earnest engagement.

(2) The "links" half of the rule ("do not just post links") is entirely unaddressed by the checklist, creating systematic false negatives for the most obvious type of violation (bare link dumps). (3) The anchor is drawn from the moderator's extended description rather than the compiler-visible rule text. The examples are notably better than the checklist itself and actually test the missing "bare links" dimension, highlighting the coverage gap.

The major issue is the anchor accuracy: the compiler had only "No plagiarism" as the rule text, so any anchor claiming to quote from it cannot be a meaningful verbatim substring beyond those two words. The anchor "directly copying and pasting another person's words and trying to pass them off as your own" is lifted from the moderator's description, which the compiler may or may not have had available. Everything else about the compiled checklist is high quality — the rubric is detailed, the threshold is justified, and the examples are well-constructed.

This is an exceptionally well-compiled single-item checklist for a nuanced intellectual fallacy rule. The prompt_template and rubric are notably thorough — far exceeding what is typically seen in content moderation checklists. The atmosphere_note correctly calibrates the threshold for AskHistorians' known standards. The main minor observation is that the three fallacies could theoretically be broken into three separate child items (one per fallacy) to allow more granular flagging, but the single-item approach is defensible given they are all of the same conceptual type and the rubric already differentiates them. No significant issues in any dimension.

The anchor accuracy issue is structural: the compiler was given only "Jokes and humour" as the rule text, yet the anchor quotes a full sentence from the moderator's description. This suggests the compiler likely had access to the moderator's description during compilation, or the anchor was fabricated/sourced from outside the supplied rule text. In production, if the system strictly validates anchors against the rule text field, this checklist would fail anchor validation entirely. However, the substantive quality of the compiled item is excellent — it correctly interprets the rule's intent despite the terse rule text, and produces a well-structured, actionable checklist. The anchor field is the only significant deficiency.

The compilation is impressively well-crafted given the minimal rule text ("Digression") provided to the compiler. The compiler correctly inferred the full rule from context and produced a single, well-calibrated subjective item with a detailed rubric. The atmosphere_note correctly captures the nuance that the rule calls for redirection rather than removal. The only marginal improvement would be adding a second borderline example or a less obvious violating case to stress-test the threshold more thoroughly.

The most critical issue is the exact duplication of the single checklist item, which results in a tree with two identical nodes. This should be flagged as a compilation error. Additionally, the rule's specific forbidden phrases ("I'm not sure if this is true..." and "Someone will correct me if I'm wrong.") would benefit from a deterministic checklist item rather than being absorbed into a general subjective check. The rule text provided to the compiler was just a title ("Follow-up questions") with no body, so the compiler had to rely entirely on the moderator description, which may explain why only one semantic requirement was captured.

The compilation is high quality overall. The compiler correctly inferred the full rule intent from a very sparse rule title ("Pictures, videos and memes") and produced a well-calibrated single-item checklist. The atmosphere note appropriately references the AskHistorians community norm of in-depth written answers to justify the 0.65 threshold. The main area for improvement would be adding a second violating example that is less obvious (e.g., a video-only comment or a gif with a thin explanatory sentence) to better test the boundary.

rule_text_short: Directly link to published peer-reviewed research articles or a brief media summ
notes: The most critical issue is the exact duplication of the checklist item. This appears to be a compilation artifact (perhaps a copy-paste error) that would cause the rule to be evaluated twice with identical logic, wasting resources and potentially double-flagging posts. The tree should be reduced to a single item. Additionally, the item description's double-negative phrasing ("non-peer-reviewed, non-media-summary source") could be rewritten more clearly, e.g., "Does the link go to a source that is neither a peer-reviewed article nor a recognized media summary?" The rubric's mention of preprint servers (biorxiv, medrxiv, arxiv) as acceptable is a reasonable interpretation of the rule, though the rule text itself only mentions "published peer-reviewed research articles" — preprints are technically not peer-reviewed, which is a slight over-expansion of the rule's scope that isn't flagged in the rubric.

rule_text_short: No summaries of summaries, reviews or popular reposts (over 100 upvotes).
notes: The core flaw in this compilation is the misapplication of the karma threshold. The rule bans summaries-of-summaries and reviews unconditionally, and only applies the ">100 upvotes" condition to reposts. The compiled tree incorrectly treats the upvote threshold as applying to all three content types, which would allow summaries-of-summaries and reviews with low karma to pass through. The tree also needs separate child nodes for each of the three content types to properly model the rule's logic. The `action: "continue"` with no children effectively makes the tree produce no actionable outcome.

rule_text_short: Research must be less than 6 months old.
notes: The most notable issue is the `item_type` classification as "structural." A structural check implies the system can resolve it from post metadata alone (e.g., post flair, timestamp of the post itself). However, `publication_date_days_ago` refers to the publication date of the linked research, which is not a native Reddit metadata field — it would need to be extracted from the linked article, making this more of a subjective or content-inspection check. If this system supports automated extraction of linked-article publication dates, then "structural" would be appropriate; but that assumption should be validated. The 183-day approximation for 6 months is defensible but introduces a small systematic error at the boundary (up to ~3 days).

rule_text_short: Do not request personal info, including pics.
notes: The most significant issue is the "deterministic" classification of the sole checklist item. While regex patterns can catch many surface-level violations, this rule inherently requires contextual judgment — e.g., "where are you from?" could be requesting location (personal info) in some contexts, or simply conversational in others. Classifying it as deterministic may lead to both false positives (innocent phrasing that matches the regex) and false negatives (violations phrased in ways the regex doesn't cover). A "subjective" or hybrid classification would be more appropriate. The compiled checklist is otherwise minimal, clear, and well-anchored for such a short rule.

rule_text_short: Do not mention upvotes or downvotes. We do not allow vote manipulation.
notes: The most significant issue is the logical conflation of "do not mention" (absolute, regardless of intent) with "vote manipulation" (implies intentional coordination). The rule text treats these as two separate prohibitions, but the compiled tree collapses them into one deterministic check. This means the tree will flag incidental mentions (e.g., "I got downvoted for saying X") the same as explicit solicitation, which may or may not match moderator intent. Splitting into two items — one for bare mentions and one for manipulation intent — would improve both logical correctness and clarity. The borderline example in the generated examples inadvertently references a second checklist item description ("Is the mention of upvotes/downvotes clearly a request...") that does not exist in the compiled tree, suggesting the compiler considered but didn't implement this split.

Additionally, the first example's `related_checklist_item_description` ("Does the post or comment contain disrespectful, rude, or abusive language directed at another person?") does not match either checklist item's actual description, suggesting the example was generated referencing a different version of the checklist or a deterministic item that was removed.
